lesson.title: Continuity & IVT
lesson.body: A function f is continuous at a point a if three conditions hold: f(a) is defined, lim_{x→a} f(x) exists, and lim_{x→a} f(x) = f(a). Discontinuities come in three types: removable (a hole the limit exists), jump (one-sided limits are unequal), and infinite (a vertical asymptote). The Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT) states that if f is continuous on [a, b] and N is any value strictly between f(a) and f(b), then there exists at least one c in (a, b) with f(c) = N — guaranteeing roots can be located within intervals.

rooms.question: Which three conditions must hold for f to be continuous at x = a?
steps: All three conditions must hold simultaneously.
answers: f'(a) exists, f(a) > 0, and the graph is smooth
f(a) is defined, lim_{x→a} f(x) exists, and lim_{x→a} f(x) = f(a)
f(a) is defined and f is differentiable at a
lim_{x→a} f(x) exists and equals zero
rooms.explanation: Continuity at a requires: (1) f(a) defined, (2) the limit exists, and (3) the limit equals f(a). All three must hold.

rooms.question: f(x) = (x²-9)/(x-3) has a __ discontinuity at x = 3.
steps: The limit as x→3 exists (equals 6), but f(3) is undefined.
rooms.explanation: The limit exists (= 6) but f(3) is undefined — a hole in the graph. This is a removable discontinuity, fixable by defining f(3) = 6.

rooms.question: f(x) = ⌊x⌋ (floor function) has what type of discontinuity at every integer?
steps: Consider lim_{x→n⁻} ⌊x⌋ and lim_{x→n⁺} ⌊x⌋ for integer n.
The left-hand limit is n-1, the right-hand limit is n.
rooms.explanation: At each integer n, the left-hand limit is n-1 and the right-hand limit is n, so the two one-sided limits differ — a jump discontinuity.

rooms.question: f is continuous on [1, 4], f(1) = -2, f(4) = 5. By the IVT, there exists c in (1, 4) with f(c) = ?
steps: The IVT guarantees f takes every value between f(1) = -2 and f(4) = 5.
Any value N with -2 < N < 5 is guaranteed.
answers: Nothing is guaranteed unless f is differentiable
rooms.explanation: The IVT guarantees every intermediate value between f(a) and f(b) is attained, i.e., every N in (-2, 5).

rooms.question: Order the steps to verify f is continuous at x = 2 for f(x) = x² - 1:
items: Check that lim_{x→2} f(x) = f(2), confirming continuity
rooms.explanation: First confirm f(2) is defined, compute its value, compute the limit, then check they agree.

boss.name: The Discontinuity
boss.taunt: I am the hole, the jump, the pole — I shatter your function at every turn. Prove the limit exists if you can!

questions.question: Which value of k makes f(x) = { kx² for x < 2; 3x+2 for x ≥ 2 } continuous at x = 2?
questions.explanation: For continuity: lim_{x→2⁻} kx² = 4k and f(2) = 3(2)+2 = 8. Set 4k = 8 → k = 2.

questions.question: f(x) = 1/(x-5). At x = 5, this is a(n) __ discontinuity.
questions.explanation: As x→5, |1/(x-5)| → ∞. The function has a vertical asymptote at x = 5 — an infinite discontinuity.

questions.question: g(x) = x³ - x - 1. The IVT guarantees a root on which interval?
questions.explanation: g(1) = 1-1-1 = -1 < 0 and g(2) = 8-2-1 = 5 > 0. Since g is continuous and changes sign on [1,2], IVT guarantees a root there.
